rationale: The cited paper is used in multiple functional places, not just a background list: as prior automobile loyalty modeling work, as empirical precedent on demographic effects (e.g., age), and as a benchmark for interpreting the satisfaction-loyalty link. The citing paper explicitly compares its own estimates to Mittal and Kamakura (2001), noting consistency on direction but arguing that models that ignore replacement timing/nonstationarity can overstate satisfaction effects. This gives the citation a meaningful role in framing and in qualifying interpretation of prior findings.
relation_description: The citing paper treats Mittal and Kamakura (2001) as an important benchmark on satisfaction and repurchase behavior, then qualifies that line of results by adding interpurchase timing and nonstationary hazards. It argues this added structure refines inference by showing the satisfaction effect can be biased upward when timing is omitted.
